narrative_blocks.header: Manchester farmer and 'strong Anti-Slavery man'
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney farmed at Manchester Connecticut his entire adult life and was described in EH as a 'strong Anti-Slavery man.' He married Edna Alice Adams in 1824; she descended from William Bradford, second governor of Plymouth Colony. Their son James Bradford Olcott carried the Bradford name as a middle name forward — and the family abolition values into the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Manchester farmer and 'strong Anti-Slavery man'
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney farmed at Manchester Connecticut his entire adult life and was described in EH as a 'strong Anti-Slavery man.' He married Edna Alice Adams in 1824; she descended from William Bradford, second governor of Plymouth Colony. Their son James Bradford Olcott carried the Bradford name as a middle name forward — and the family abolition values into the next generation.

birth.note: FindAGrave alt date 1800; using 1809 from family Bradford source records.

parents.father_research_notes: Father James Adams of Plainfield CT.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Alice Bradford Adams — descendant of William Bradford (second governor of Plymouth Colony) per family records. William Bradford not built into tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Third Cynthia in a four-generation chain
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia carries her grandmother Cynthia Hooker Olcott's name forward — making her the third Cynthia in the line. Her own granddaughter through this Vail marriage continued the pattern, four generations of Cynthias tracing back to her great-grandfather Roger Hooker's daughter.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost husband and son Bradford to disease
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia outlived her husband Egbert by six years — he died of smallpox in March 1905. Their second son James Bradford Vail had predeceased both parents, dying of diphtheria in North Dakota in August 1902 at age 40. Cynthia herself lived until April 1911, age 84.

naming_inspiration.description: Both first and middle names honor her grandmother Cynthia Hooker Olcott — the second of four generations of Cynthias in the line.

birth.note: EH says 'b. Sept. 15, 1823, at Manchester, Conn.' but FindAGrave gives Blooming Grove NY — using the more specific FindAGrave data.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician killed by smallpox at eighty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Egbert practiced medicine and farmed at Manchester for half a century. He died of smallpox in March 1905 at age 81 — a physician taken by the disease he had spent his career treating in others. Buried West Cemetery Manchester alongside his wife Cynthia, who outlived him by six years.

narrative_blocks.header: DAR member; New Britain Herald obituary 1930
narrative_blocks.body: Fanny lived most of her life at 47 Murray Street New Britain CT, a member of the Daughters of the American Revolution and the First Congregational Church. Her 1930 New Britain Herald obituary survives, listing son Worthing Vail Snow and grandsons Worthing and Olcott Snow of Bristol — the Olcott name preserved a fourth time across the generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Alice died young
narrative_blocks.body: Alice died in 1861 at the age of one.

narrative_blocks.header: Diphtheria killed him at forty in Dakota
narrative_blocks.body: James Bradford spent his last 18 years in Duluth Minnesota and the Dakotas with a western land company; he died of diphtheria in Chaffee North Dakota on 22 August 1902 at age 40, leaving his Duluth wife Cornelia Field Vail and four young children — two sons and two daughters. The Hartford Courant obituary called him 'a man of literary habits, much business ability and of an unusually clean and upright character.'

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name passed forward through three generations from his great-grandmother Alice Bradford Adams (William Bradford Plymouth Governor's descendant) — through her daughter Edna Adams Olcott to her son James Bradford Olcott to his nephew James Bradford Vail.

narrative_blocks.header: St. Louis surgeon endorsed Paine's Celery Compound
narrative_blocks.body: William edited the St. Louis Mayfield Sanitarium's Hospital Bulletin and as visiting surgeon there wrote a widely-circulated 1900s endorsement of Paine's Celery Compound — the patent-medicine equivalent of a serious medical endorsement of its day. The piece appeared as professional advertising across newspapers, citing his cases of nervous-system collapse cured by the proprietary remedy.

sources.label: Connecticut State Library — James Bradford Olcott Papers

notable.notable_blurb: Turfgrass pioneer; introduced zoysiagrass to America

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed for the Gold Rush at age eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: In 1849 James B. sailed from Connecticut to San Francisco aboard the Henry Lee as part of the California Gold Rush, age eighteen. He worked for an import company specializing in exotic Pacific plants, ran a tavern in Sacramento, then sailed around the world collecting grass specimens — visiting Hawaii, Korea, Britain, France, and Germany.

narrative_blocks.header: Introduced zoysiagrass to the United States
narrative_blocks.body: From 1889 under the auspices of the Connecticut Agricultural Experiment Station, James B. began an obsessive turfgrass research project at his Manchester farm 'The Ranch.' One acre held more than 1,500 distinct grass specimens in five-square-foot plots, which he mowed and maintained using tools of his own invention. He traveled to Europe, Australia, and New Zealand collecting samples, and was the first to introduce zoysiagrass to America.

narrative_blocks.header: Advised Olmsted on Central Park and Bushnell lawns
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick Law Olmsted consulted James B. Olcott on the lawns of his most famous landscape designs — New York's Central Park and Hartford's Bushnell Park. Olcott's reputation as 'the Grass Man' brought him into the inner circle of the most influential landscape architect of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding trustee of the Storrs Agricultural School
narrative_blocks.body: In 1880 James B. served on a three-person Connecticut General Assembly committee — with William Henry Brewer of Yale and George A. Bowen — that reviewed Charles and Augustus Storrs's offer to donate land and money for a state agricultural school. He was appointed to the school's first board of trustees in 1881 and stepped down in 1888 after advocating Yale retain its land-grant status.

narrative_blocks.header: Worked for Cheney Brothers and the Connecticut Grange
narrative_blocks.body: Between his farm and his turfgrass work, James B. oversaw outdoor work — landscaping and road construction — for the Cheney Brothers silk-manufacturer company town in South Manchester. He co-founded the Manchester chapter of the Connecticut State Grange in 1885; some peers thought him 'something of a crank.'

cross_connections.display_label: Advised Olmsted on Central Park and Bushnell Park lawns

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his maternal grandmother Alice Bradford's surname forward — the line tracing back to William Bradford, second governor of Plymouth Colony.

education.notes: Harwinton CT. Attended after returning from California.

career.notes: Inherited and managed family farm. Retired 1890; turned over to son Walter.
Wrote agriculture column for two decades.
Oversaw landscaping and road construction in the Cheney Brothers company town.

career.organization: Storrs Agricultural School (later University of Connecticut)
career.notes: Served on the original three-person committee (with William Henry Brewer of Yale and George A. Bowen) that recommended the legislature accept Charles and Augustus Storrs's 1880 land-and-money donation. Subsequently appointed to the school's first board of trustees in 1881; resigned 1888 after advocating Yale retain land-grant status.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding trustee of the Storrs Agricultural School (later University of Connecticut); served on the original review committee 1880 alongside William Henry Brewer of Yale.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Isaac Roberts of East Hartford CT.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother not preserved in available sources.

narrative_blocks.header: Margaret died young
narrative_blocks.body: Margaret died in 1860 at the age of two.

narrative_blocks.header: Charles died young
narrative_blocks.body: Charles died in 1862 at the age of two.

narrative_blocks.header: Ednah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Ednah died in 1866 at the age of one.

naming_inspiration.description: First and middle names honor her grandmother Edna Alice Adams Olcott — the Bradford-line Olcott matriarch from whom the family abolitionist tradition descends.

burial.plot_notes: Cheney Cemetery, Manchester CT — buried alongside his wife Adeline Cheney Olcott in the Cheney family burial ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited 'The Ranch' from his turfgrass father
narrative_blocks.body: When James B. Olcott retired from active farm management in 1890 he turned over The Ranch — the Manchester family farm with its acre of 1,500 turfgrass specimens — to Walter as eldest surviving son. Walter married into the Cheney silk-manufacturing family of Manchester through his wife Adeline Louise Cheney; he is buried in the Cheney family cemetery alongside her.

cross_connections.display_label: Named after his great-granduncle who died young

naming_inspiration.description: Carries forward the name of his grandfather's brother Walter Hooker Olcott (1802-1832)— the youngest of Rev. Allen Olcott and Cynthia Hooker Olcott's children, who died at age thirty without marrying. Walter the second was born thirty-five years after his namesake's death.

parents.father_research_notes: Captain Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.
parents.mother_research_notes: Penelope Beckwith Olcott, wife of Capt. Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.

burial.plot_notes: Bolton Center Cemetery, Bolton, Tolland County, Connecticut. Cenotaphic inscription also on Olcott family monument in West Cemetery, Manchester CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Yale-educated 'quaint and erratic' preacher
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia married Rev. Allen Olcott in 1792 — a Yale College 1768 graduate settled at the Farmington Presbyterian Church in 1787. After four years his preaching was deemed 'quaint and erratic' enough that he was dismissed and never settled at any other church.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled with Allen on a South Manchester farm
narrative_blocks.body: After his Farmington dismissal Allen returned to his native East Hartford and then settled with Cynthia on a farm at South Manchester, Connecticut. Family stories suggest his farming methods were as unique as his preaching had been. He died there in April 1811, leaving Cynthia a widow with five children ranging from eight to eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Capt. Samuel Alvord at fifty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Allen's death Cynthia remarried — Capt. Samuel Alvord of East Hartford, on 14 January 1813. The couple lived at East Hartford until her own death in June 1827. Through her first marriage's daughter Cynthia, her line married back into the Talcott-Hooker convergence.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut
